response_text:
# The 11:47

The bus came whether you deserved it or not. That was the first thing I learned.

It arrived at the corner of Meridian and Fifth at 11:47 on weeknights, give or take four minutes depending on whatever had happened earlier in the city — a fender bender on the connector, a medical situation at the Civic Center stop, the slow accordion of delay that could compress or stretch a schedule without warning or apology. I stood at that corner so many times that my body developed a kind of liturgy around it: shift my bag to the left shoulder, check the app even though the app was often wrong, look north up the avenue for the yellow headlights before the number appeared on the destination board.

I was working late that whole year. I want to be honest about why, because the reason matters. It wasn't ambition. It was avoidance. My apartment was quiet in a way that felt accusatory after my partner moved out in February, and the office — fluorescent, cluttered, warm with the sound of the HVAC system — was easier to be inside than an empty place where someone else's absence had a shape. So I stayed until ten, sometimes eleven, and then I walked four blocks to Meridian and Fifth and I waited for the 11:47, which was the last express on that route until morning.

---

The regulars sorted themselves out within the first month. I noticed them the way you notice recurring figures in a dream — not all at once, but gradually, with a growing certainty that you have been here before.

There was the man I thought of as the Contractor. He boarded two stops after me, always in Carhartt canvas pants stiffened with dried concrete or drywall compound, always carrying a hard hat under his arm like a clutch purse. He had forearms the size of my thighs and he always fell asleep before the third stop, tilting imperceptibly toward the window until his forehead touched the glass with a gentleness
